data.title: RF Industries: Better Margins, Better Demand, Better Stock
data.description:
I thought RF Industries (RFIL) was worth a second look as I researched it, because this stock seems uninteresting at first. For starters, it’s a small company that designs and makes interconnected products. RF Industries sells products that enable communication and industrial systems to operate in the background, like cable assemblies, connectors, custom wiring, small cell systems, and cooling products.

It mostly means making the hardware that keeps networks and other infrastructure running. It may not sound very interesting, but companies that do this can still be good investments because these things are foundational. Nobody can live without having their smartphones nearby, and as the world moves towards globalization, it's companies like these that don’t really have the spotlight, and I think that’s where value truly lies.

Fiscal 2025 was a much better year for the company. Revenue grew by 24.2% to reach $80.6 million, gross margin rose by 29.1% to 33.2%, and the operating income recorded a gain after being negative last year.

I believe that a Buy rating for RFIL stock can be justified by the fact that it has been improving, and more to the point, I continue to believe that the stock is undervalued. My opinion is anchored on three factors: that margins are improving, there seems to be expansion in the demand over products and end markets, and the current valuation does not completely reflect the business recovery to steady profitability.

What the Business Does

RF Industries sells hardware that helps networks and other systems connect and operate, which includes cable assemblies, RF connectors, wiring harnesses, fiber-related products, small-cell systems, and cooling products. I believe that the product mix of RF Industries’ products is important, as it is not limited to a single level of the connectivity stack. As indicated in the 10-K, the company does not just fit into a single, small category; it markets RF connectors, adapters, coaxial and data cables, wire harnesses, fiber optic products, custom cabling, cooling systems, and integrated small-cell enclosures.

That breadth plays an operationally significant role as it provides the company with various opportunities to engage in customer deployments rather than employing only one product family, making them more competitive than a simple catalog-parts supplier, even though some of the categories remain obviously competitive.

I believe that the connector and cable sides remain foundational, although it is most likely the least differentiated aspect of the portfolio. According to the filing, RF Industries has over 1,500 connector, adapter, tool, and test-kit types available, as well as over 100,000 cable-assembly combinations, both standard and custom.

I am reading between the lines that such a scale and size make the company relevant in a variety of uses, particularly since such products can serve thousands of devices and applications. I do not view this as the strongest moat, because the company also says the RF Connector division carries thousands of products, most of which are available in standard sizes and configurations and are also offered by competitors.

I found the highest-value integrated products of the company, particularly passive RF products, thermal control systems, and small-cell enclosures, to be the most interesting area in the product range. The 10-K reports that Microlab produces passive RF and microwave components such as dividers, directional couplers, and filters used in DAS, wireless base stations, and small-cell networks, and Schrofftech produces intelligent thermal cooling systems and pole-ready small-cell shrouds and enclosures. I believe that the categories are more strategically significant since they are addressing signal management and installation and operating environment issues instead of simply addressing a simple connector request, which to me is a good indicator that RF Industries is attempting to shift towards products that are in closer proximity to the system-level problem of the customer, which precisely is the direction I would like to see.

To me, that is one sign that RF Industries is trying to move closer to the customer’s broader system-level problem. That interpretation also lines up with management’s more recent language on the Q4 2025 earnings call, where executives described the business as evolving from a component supplier toward a more solutions-oriented model.

Why Fiscal 2025 Mattered

RFIL’s revenue rose to $80.36 million, which was up 24.2% from the prior year; gross profit increased to $26.14 million, and gross margin rose to 33.2% from 29.1%. Operating income also improved to $1.8 million from an operating loss of $2.8 million in fiscal 2024, indicating that in 2025 the company had better scale, a better mix, and better execution.

During the Q4 2025 call, CFO Peter Yin indicated that gross margin increased to 37% in the fourth quarter, compared to 31%, and that the growth was brought about by increasing sales and a more friendly product mix.

The management also mentioned the high contribution of integrated systems like small cell and DAC thermal cooling, and full-year 2025 results were propelled by its shipments of the custom cabling business to one of the major aerospace customers.

The increase of gross margin by 250 basis points over the year in Q1 2026 to 32.3% was still reported to be price realization, efficiency in its operations, and control of costs, which were said by management. And that to me is the most obvious indication that RF Industries is not merely enjoying the benefits of volume but of selling more of the types of products and solutions with better economics.

Retention of customers must also be linked with something more tangible as opposed to my opinion. During the Q4 2025 call, management also indicated that it was seeking to build deeper relationships with current customers, deploy higher-value proprietary offerings as the tip of the spear, and then cross-sell more products once it was collaboratively working with the technical and market teams of the customer.

Customers are also approaching RF Industries sooner in their design cycles, which is important, the management says, since once a supplier is brought on board in the earlier stages of specification and design, the relationship is more likely to be long-lasting and the portion of wallet can increase over time.

I would not refer to that as evidence of retention in the strict numeric sense, but I believe it is reasonable evidence that the management is making an intentional effort to make customer relationships more valuable and sticky.

If revenue rose but profits stayed weak, I would’ve asked more questions, but since profits and margins improved along with revenue, no questions asked there. Alongside rising revenues and improving margins, operating cash flow totaled $4.6 million in 2025, suggesting the earnings improvement because it’s the highest it has been in 5 years.

The Latest Quarter Looks Better

Having said that, the large-project comment remains relevant since it demonstrates how RF Industries can be when it comes to the timing of projects. The management did not report that previous-year project as a repeat source of demand; instead, CEO Rob Dawson treated it as a "welcome anomaly" and so it should have been considered as a gain to the comparison time period and not a sustenance into future operating activity.

The table numbers can assist in demonstrating the importance of such a difference. It achieved $18.97 million against $19.20 million revenue in the year before, and hence the sales dropped by only 1.2% annually, which resulted in a higher profitability. EBITDA rose to $1.06 million from $0.87 million, up 22.2%, while EBITDA margin increased to 5.58% from 4.52%, a gain of 107 basis points. EBIT also improved to $0.18 million from $0.06 million, up 216.1%, and EBIT margin rose to 0.93% from 0.29%. This to me means that that current quarter was not being supported with the same type of one-off project benefit, but instead it was reporting greater underlying profitability on about similar revenue.

What is more important to future operating performance is whether the company can substitute that type of one-off benefit with more widespread and more repeatable demand.

The recent commentary by management is headed in that direction because in the Q1 2026 call, Dawson noted that backlog increased in various areas, such as small cell, DAC thermal cooling, custom cabling to aerospace, and custom cabling to industrial customers, and CFO Peter Yin reminded investors once again that backlog is just a snapshot in time and can fluctuate based on the timing and completion of orders.

This does not eliminate volatility but does indicate that the future operating performance might be less dependent on any specific large project that seems unusual as compared to the previous-year quarter.

Backlog and Demand

Backlog is improved, and the management was attentive not to oversell it. During the Q1 2026 call, CFO Peter Yin reported that the backlog at Q4 2025, based on $17.9 million in bookings, was $14.4 million and indicated an increase to $18.6 million by the time of the call. He also commented that backlog is merely a "snapshot in time" and can shift around as orders are received and processed and thus not to be used as an indicator of future revenue but merely as a general health indicator.

Management said that the rise was not due to a single order and their backlog growth was an indication of a healthy mix in small cell, DAC thermal cooling, custom cabling to aerospace, and custom cabling to industrial customers, said CEO Rob Dawson. That is important because it implies that the demand is becoming widespread throughout the business and not a single project with an exceptionally high cost.

Meanwhile, management failed to offer backlog as an indication that growth will be smooth sailing. CFO Peter Yin repeated that backlog can swing significantly between periods, and Dawson indicated that the bigger backlog helps management anticipate that revenue would be better in Q2 than in Q1 and that it would be growing thereafter. I have learned that the trend of the backlog is promising, yet it must be translated into shipments before it can be considered as solid evidence of the continued growth.

Margins and Balance Sheet

The margins are the main reason I like this stock. It's great that sales are going up, but better margins are what will really change how the market sees a company like this. The gross margin went up from 29.1% to 33.2% in fiscal 2025. In the fourth quarter of fiscal 2025, management talked about a gross margin of about 37% and an adjusted EBITDA margin of about 11.5%, which was higher than its long-term goal of 10% for adjusted EBITDA margin. If RF Industries can keep its margins in the low 30% range and keep growing its higher-value product categories, then profits can grow faster than sales. That's when a stock usually starts to change its rating. Investors usually don't pay much for a small company with uneven margins, but they will pay more for a business that is showing it can grow and make money.

I don't think the balance sheet is a big deal either. The company had $5.1 million in cash and about $7.1 million drawn on its revolver at the end of the first quarter of fiscal 2026. That means the total amount of debt was only about $2.0 million. Management also said that net debt was lower than it had been a year ago.

That level of debt seems reasonable to me for a small business that just started making money and improving its cash flow. It's not perfect, but it's not the kind of stress on the balance sheet that would make me stay away from the stock. RF Industries still needs to keep working, but it doesn't look like it's in a financial bind. That gives management some time to keep putting money into the best parts of the business and gives investors a little more peace of mind if growth doesn't happen as quickly as they thought it would.

Valuation

The first important assumption is growth, so I went with 13% revenue growth for FY2026 and 14% for FY2027. A lot of changes can steer these assumptions, like backlog improving, bookings being solid, and a broader demand instead of RFIL leaning on one big project.

In the DCF below, I also used a 34.5% gross margin for FY2026 because it's the right middle ground; it's above the 33.2% the company delivered for FY2025 but still below the 37% management talked about in Q4. In the end, this doesn't automatically mean something big will happen down the line; it's just RFIL might continue to keep its current gross margins.

This model also uses an 8% EBITDA margin in FY2026 and 10.5% in fiscal 2027 because RFIL is still seen as a small, uneven hardware business, but if it can move towards management’s long-term goal of 10%+ adjusted EBITDA margin, then I don't think the stock will stay valued as is.

Put two and two together, and these assumptions produce an EV of about $143.05m and an equity value of about $141.04m, or roughly $13.05 per share, and compare that with today's price at $10.44; the model shows a 25% upside.

To me, that upside works because it comes from a pretty simple chain of logic, and I'm not saying that RF Industries is dramatically mispriced, but if we're using FY2025 as a basis and considering the macro trends where markets have been unfavorable and the fact that they're getting broader demand, then to me, the stock still looks undervalued.

Risks

Among the criticisms that would be better discussed in more direct terms is the fact that RF Industries has historically developed a large portion of its platform by acquisition, as opposed to organic growth by itself.

The present business comprises Cables Unlimited, Rel-Tech, Schrofftech, C Enterprises, and Microlab, all of which have been acquired over the years, with Microlab in 2022 being the most recent major acquisition. Therefore, I do not believe that it would be just to portray RF Industries as an organic compounder. Meanwhile, the recent recovery does not seem to be acquisition-based in the short term, since there has not been a new big deal behind the improvement of fiscal 2025. Rather, the management has been reporting the recovery as being in terms of improved product mix, enhanced execution, expanded end-market exposure, and enhanced contribution by small cell, DAC thermal cooling, RF passive solutions, and custom cabling. I believe that the present recovery is more of an operational than an acquisitive one, but the business is small and project-based to the extent that organic growth will probably continue to be uneven quarter to quarter.

Another risk is that the company is highly reliant on wireless and broadband communications providers and cautions that any cyclical decline in the communications industry would have a significant adverse impact on demand, revenue, and profitability.

The management stated on the Q4 2025 call that telecom and wireless have reduced from about 70% of sales in previous years to about 50% today, which is significant progress, but a significant portion of the business is still linked to carrier, broadband, and other infrastructure budgets. Should those markets falter once again, particularly in a small firm where project-based income is the rule, things could go downhill fast.

The other risk is that the increase in profitability can be more volume sensitive than the market anticipates. The management has repeatedly indicated that increased sales, improved mix, and operating leverage are collaborating to push up margins.

That is positive on the uphill side, but it also implies that the model can be used in reverse in case projects are postponed, or telecom or infrastructure expenditure decelerates, or higher-value product lines like DAC, small cell, and custom cabling fail to scale. RF Industries might still be profitable in that case, but the road to a higher multiple would be a lot less obvious.

If revenue growth doesn't come back in the second half of 2026, the stock may already be closer to fair value than I would like. Still, when I look at the good things and the bad things, I tend to be on the bullish side. RFIL looks stronger, the margins look better, the demand trends look good, and the valuation still leaves room for growth. For me, that's enough to give it a Buy rating.

Conclusion

Overall, I still think RF Industries is a buy because the company is starting to show real improvement and not just small, short-term hype. The business made more money, margins got better, demand looked more spread out, and the company does not seem to be under major financial pressure. What stands out to me is that the stock still looks fairly cheap compared to the progress the company has already made. So even if it is not a perfect business, I think there is enough going right here to believe the upside still outweighs the risks.

data.title: VCR: Consumer Discretionary Sector Dashboard For April
data.description:
This monthly column provides a top-down analysis of the consumer discretionary sector, based on value, quality and momentum metrics. It may also help analyze sector ETFs such as Vanguard Consumer Discretionary ETF (VCR) and Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLY), whose largest holdings are used to calculate these metrics.

Shortcut

The next two paragraphs in italic describe the dashboard methodology. They are necessary for new readers to understand the metrics. If you are used to this series or if you are short of time, you can skip them and go to the charts.

Base Metrics

I calculate the median value of five fundamental ratios for each subsector: Earnings Yield ("EY"), Sales Yield ("SY"), Free Cash Flow Yield ("FY"), Return on Equity ("ROE"), Gross Margin ("GM"). The reference universe includes large companies in the U.S. stock market. The five base metrics are calculated on trailing 12 months. For all of them, higher is better. EY, SY and FY are medians of the inverse of Price/Earnings, Price/Sales and Price/Free Cash Flow. They are better for statistical studies than price-to-something ratios, which are unusable or non available when the "something" is close to zero or negative (for example, companies with negative earnings). I also look at two momentum metrics for each group: the median monthly return (RetM) and the median annual return (RetY).

I prefer medians to averages because a median splits a set in a good half and a bad half. A capital-weighted average is skewed by extreme values and the largest companies. My metrics are designed for stock-picking rather than index investing.

Value and Quality Scores

I calculate historical baselines for all metrics. They are noted respectively EYh, SYh, FYh, ROEh, GMh, and they are calculated as the averages on a look-back period of 11 years. For example, the value of EYh for retailing in the table below is the 11-year average of the median Earnings Yield in retail companies.

The Value Score ("VS") is defined as the average difference in % between the three valuation ratios (EY, SY, FY) and their baselines (EYh, SYh, FYh). The same way, the Quality Score ("QS") is the average difference between the two quality ratios (ROE, GM) and their baselines (ROEh, GMh).

The scores are in percentage points. VS may be interpreted as the percentage of undervaluation or overvaluation relative to the baseline (positive is good, negative is bad). This interpretation must be taken with caution: the baseline is an arbitrary reference, not a supposed fair value. The formula assumes that the three valuation metrics are of equal importance.A floor of -100 is set for VS and QS when the calculation goes below this value. It may happen when metrics in a subsector are very bad.

For example, the EY column tells us that a retail company with an Earnings Yield above 0.0461 (or price/earnings below 21.69) is in the better half of the industry regarding this metric. data.title: Tenaz Energy: An Important Call Option On European Natural Gas Prices
data.description:
Introduction

Now that the oil and European gas prices are normalizing after the Iran-related price run, I want to have another look at Tenaz Energy (TNZ:CA) (ATUUF). I have been keeping track of this company for quite a while now, but the anticipated production growth in 2026 and 2027 may catch the market by surprise (although clearly signaled by the company). I have always maintained a long-term European natural gas price of 25-35 EUR per MWh, and Tenaz Energy should benefit from that price range.

A Look at the Expectations for 2026

For the current financial year, the company anticipates to produce between 19,500 and 22,500 barrels of oil equivalent per day. I would like to emphasize that this is just the average, as the company anticipates to exit this year with an attributable production that could be as high as 27,000 barrels of oil equivalent per day.

The production growth comes on the back of acquisitions that were completed in 2025, while a total of 3.1 to 3.6 net new wells will be drilled in the Netherlands (in a combination of wells drilled on the operated assets as well as on the non-operated assets). Additionally, the company will drill three wells in Canada, representing a net attributable well count of 2.6.

And while total production is expressed in barrels of oil equivalent, this actually is a natural gas company, as 93% of the anticipated production mix for this year will consist of natural gas. And the vast majority of this is European natural gas, subject to the European TTF natural gas price (which is a multiple of the North American natural gas price, with current prices north of US$10/mmbtu).

A key advantage of operating offshore in the Netherlands is the fact that there is no royalty payable, and although the statutory tax rate of 46% is high, it represents approximately 1/4 of the pre-tax funds from operations. This makes the total tax pressure quite manageable and not as prohibitive as, for instance, UK North Sea operations.

Unfortunately (although it is good practice), the company has hedged the majority of its anticipated natural gas production in Europe at an average in the low €30 range per MWh, while approximately 30% of the 2027 output has been hedged as well. But as you can see below, there's plenty of room to add more hedges for both 2027 as well as starting to roll out hedges for 2028 now that the TTF price levels remain at solid levels.

One of the key elements I will be looking forward to when the company publishes its results for the first quarter of the current financial year is to see whether or not it took advantage of the very strong TTF natural gas prices to lock in additional hedges. Just to give you an example, the TTF price for delivery in Q3 2027 was trading above 40 EUR per MWh in the past few weeks, so this would have been a good opportunity to increase the ceiling price in the hedge book.

I anticipate the total production in 2027 to come in at approximately 30,000 to 32,500 barrels of oil equivalent per day (with natural gas still representing almost the entire oil equivalent output). Using a TTF natural gas price of C$13/mcf and C$81/barrel for oil, Tenaz expects a total FFO of approximately C$500M, and in combination with a lower capex requirement of around C$175-200M, this could pave the way to C$300M in net free cash flow.

That would mean that the total amount of net debt (which stood at almost C$350M as of the end of last year) should evaporate fast, and with a little bit of luck, the company will be debt-free by the end of next year (subject to the contingent consideration payments as part of the recent acquisitions). This could pave the way to pursue more M&A (keep in mind the management team is the team that built Vermilion Energy (VET) (VET:CA) from the ground up) with Tenaz pursuing a roll-up strategy.

The Reserves: Okay, but Could Be Higher

One key element that could have some room for improvement is the total amount of reserves on the assets. While the company touts a reserve life index of 16 years based on the 2P reserves (shown below), this number is based on the 2025 production numbers. Once the consolidated output increases to in excess of 30,000 barrels of oil equivalent per day, this reserve life index will drop to a [high] single-digit number.

This also means the current market cap and enterprise value of Tenaz exceed the after-tax PV10 value of the cash flows (which stood at C$1.6B as of the end of last year). After deducting the almost C$350M in net debt, the PV10 value comes in at C$1.25B, or around C$40 per share (rounded). The PV10 calculation is based on a TTF natural gas price of just over US$10/mmbtu in the next three years. So a step-up in the TTF natural gas price will bring cash flows forward and reduce the impact of the cumulative discount rate.

While not a deterrent at this moment, it would be good to see Tenaz working on converting some of its resources into a 2P reserve category to ensure the reserve life index remains above 10 years, even at the increased production rate.

Investment Thesis

Tenaz’s management has made it quite clear that M&A is a very realistic possibility (it ‘has a very good transaction pipeline in place’), and I am looking forward to see what kind of assets Tenaz is able to acquire and at what cost.

While I think this year’s net free cash flow will remain below C$100M due to a capex push and a rather moderate production increase, expectations for 2027 are much higher. I wouldn't be surprised to see a total net free cash flow of approximately C$300M based on a 30-35 EUR/MWh TTF natural gas price in 2027, which would represent C$9/share based on the current share count.

I currently have no position in the company, but I will be looking to establish an initial long position in the next few weeks, taking advantage of the current weakness in its share price.

data.title: Alamar Biosciences: Looking For An Early Detection
data.description:
Shares of Alamar Biosciences (ALMR) have seen a very successful public offering. Shares have risen 30% on the first day of trading, with investors being very upbeat on its precision proteomics technology for early disease detection. I like the mission of the business a lot, its potential as well, as the same applies for the sales momentum demonstrated upon.

While some operating deleverage has been seen in the first quarter, overall momentum is strong, as I do not fear the losses given these strong cash balances. The strong sales momentum is very welcome, and while expectations have become a bit more demanding, they are not outrageous given the prospects here.

For now I am keeping a very close eye on the future reports of the business, looking to learn more about the topline growth trends, and the impact on the bottom line, before considering a position here.

Other interesting IPOs are discussed more extensively at Value In Corporate Events.

Precision Proteomics For Early Disease Detection

Alamar has a mission to power precision to enable the earliest detection of disease. The company was founded by Yuling Luo, a serial founder and entrepreneur in the life science business. In a family event, he recognizes that the most effective treatment for patients suffering from cancer and other diseases is that of detection, with proteins playing an instrumental role in that.

Based on this insight, the business was founded in 2018. With detection being key in the early part of the diseases, this is just the time when the biological signals are the weakest. That results in many false positives, but the company finds a solid balance in this, creating real insights. Currently such solutions are only used in research settings, not authorized for clinical usage yet, but of course being applied in such settings is among the goals of the business here.

The company positions itself as conservative, claiming a culture and playbook of under-promising and over-delivering, committed to building a principled culture and building the company in stages.

The company has precisely built its NULISA technology to address limitations of existing proteomics tools by detecting protein biomarkers at very low concentrations in non-invasive fluids. The ARGO High Throughput (HT) system was launched in January 2024, and by now has been used by more than 300 customers across 25 countries. Besides this system, the company provides consumables and services as well.

Valuation & IPO Thoughts

Alamar aimed to sell 9.4 million shares in a preliminary price range between $15 and $17 per share, with pricing set at the high end of the range at $17 per share. In total, some 11.2 million shares were sold by the company, which ended up fetching $191 million in gross proceeds.

With some 66.5 million shares outstanding following the offering, the company is awarded a $1.13 billion equity valuation at the offer price. The company operates with a net cash position of just over a quarter of a billion, for an operating asset valuation just below $900 million here.

The company reported revenues just north of $25 million in 2024, these revenues being accompanied by a nearly $50 million operating loss. Real gains have been seen in 2025, with revenue essentially tripling to $74 million, while operating losses narrowed to $31 million.

The company has not reported revenues for each quarter, yet the preliminary numbers for the first quarter of 2025 reveal that the first quarter revenues for 2025 came in at $13 million. More important is that first-quarter revenues for 2026 are seen around $25.7 million, trending around a hundred million each year. So far so good, as the business is trading around 9 times sales here, these revenues nearly doubling on an annual basis.

Operating losses are seen ticking up to a midpoint of $12.6 million, trending around $50 million each year here, suggesting some deleveraging from the 2025 results, albeit that the net cash holdings are substantial enough to make investors not worry about this.

Shares have risen by nearly 30% on the first day of trading, now exchanging hands at $22 per share. This has pushed up the operating asset valuation to more than $1.2 billion, pushing up annualized sales multiples to around 12 times sales here, this now still being a loss-making business.

Concluding Thoughts

Risks in the offering are plentiful, including demanding valuations, losses being reported, reliance on a single product, reliance on key staff, changes in the regulatory field, changes in competitive technologies, etc.

Peers include smaller, focused names like Quanterix (QTRX), which has been struggling in a huge manner. Olink, owned by Thermo Fisher (TMO), is part of a much larger and diversified peer.

This makes it very difficult to make realistic comparisons here, as the business is a bit unique in its form. I like the business a great deal, its mission, and its sales momentum. While I am not chasing the shares right now, I am perfectly happy to keep a very close eye on the trends going forward.
